The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Onneto|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|onsen composition|ja|泉質}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.

{{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Sulfur]] [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Quasi-drugs]] [[Category:Beppu]] [[Category:Kusatsu]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{citation needed|Yunohana (mineral deposits)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{citation needed|Yunohana (mineral deposits)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{citation needed|Yunohana (mineral deposits)}} [[Category:Yuno-hana]][[Category:Yuno-hana]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:温泉街]] [[ja:湯の花]]

[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Onneto|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|onsen composition|ja|泉質}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン}} * {{ill|Water bloom|ja|水の華}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花}}

By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花}}
{{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Sulfur]] [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Quasi-drugs]] [[Category:Beppu]] [[Category:Kusatsu]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yunohana (mineral deposits)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yunohana (mineral deposits)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yunohana (mineral deposits)}} [[Category:Yuno-hana]][[Category:Yuno-hana]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:温泉街]] [[ja:湯の花]]

[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Onneto|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}

For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花}}

[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}

There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.

{{Onsen-stub}} {{Onsen}} [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Sulfur]] [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Quasi-drugs]] [[Category:Beppu]] [[Category:Kusatsu]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yunohana (mineral deposits)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Yunohana (mineral deposits)-qq]] [[Category:Yunohana (mineral deposits)]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yunohana (mineral deposits)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Yunohana (mineral deposits)-qq]] [[Category:Yunohana (mineral deposits)]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yunohana (mineral deposits)}} [[Category:Yuno-hana]][[Category:Yuno-hana]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:温泉街]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yunohana (mineral deposits)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:湯の花]]

[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.

These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.

The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />

Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花}}

[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}

Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花}}

revision.text:
[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}

Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.

The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.

The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />

=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>

There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.

[[Category:qq]][[sn:Yunohana (mineral deposits)]][[Category: :qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yunohana (mineral deposits)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
