At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー|12=draft}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|12=draft}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街|1=spa town|lt=Onsen town|12=draft}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|12=draft}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|12=draft}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|12=draft}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|12=draft}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}} == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン|12=draft}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花|12=draft}}
{{Onsen-stub}} {{citation needed|Yunohana}} <!--Yunohana--> {{citation needed|Yunohana}} <!--Yunohana--> {{citation needed|Yunohana}}

{{draft categories| [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Sulfur]] [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Quasi-drugs]] [[Category:Beppu]] [[Category:Kusatsu]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1etion categories]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Yunohana]] [[Category:Yuno-hana]] [[Category:温泉街]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[en:Yunohana]] [[ja:湯の花]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yunohana}}

[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=draft}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|12=draft}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|1=Onsen|1=Onsen|12=draft}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|12=draft}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|12=draft}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|12=draft}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー|12=draft}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|12=draft}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街|1=spa town|lt=Onsen town|12=draft}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|12=draft}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|12=draft}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|12=draft}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|12=draft}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}} == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン|12=draft}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花|12=draft}}

revision.text:
[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|[[那須温泉郷]][[殺生石]]近郊産の湯の花（源泉付近の含硫黄堆積物）。黄色い部分は硫黄の斑晶。]] '''湯の花'''（ゆのはな）とは、[[温泉]]の不溶性成分が[[析出]]・[[沈殿]]したものを指す{{Sfn|上野ら(2008)}}。「湯の花」以外にも、湯花、湯の華、湯華など、複数の表記がある。一般に[[入浴剤]]などの用途で採取・販売されている。
== 概要 == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|湯の花採取光景（湯畑）（[[玉川温泉 (秋田県)|玉川温泉]]）]] 高温で湧出した[[源泉]]が大気に接触すると、温度差による冷却（[[溶解度]]の減少）、[[溶媒]]成分の蒸発、[[酸素]]との反応などにより源泉中の温泉成分や混在していた物質の沈殿が発生する。この不溶性沈殿が湯の花である{{Sfn|上野ら(2008)}}。析出物沈殿物は、粒子状になって浴槽の底に沈んだり、糸状になって浴槽内を漂ったり、浴槽の壁面や湯口に付着する。浴槽内を漂う湯の花は汚れと誤解されることもあるため、注意書きを掲示している温泉施設もある。<!--[[File:Owani Onsen Aomori 02.jpg|thumb|湯の花の注意書き（[[大鰐温泉]]）]]←この写真は言語の著作物に対する権利侵害にあたるのではないか。-->
また、湯の花は、温泉地においては温泉成分の沈殿物のみならず、硫黄鉱山原石粉砕品、源泉湧出地の石粉砕品、噴煙等粘土かぶせ湯花（別府温泉・奥塩原温泉が有名）、[[蒸気]]が付着したものなど多数の可能性がある。
[[北海道]]にある[[オンネトー]]のマンガンや、[[富山県]]にある[[新湯]]の玉滴石（[[オパール]]）など、湯の花が「生きている鉱床」となることがある。
湯の花を集めて包装したものは、温泉地における[[土産]]の定番として広く流通しており、多くの[[温泉街]]で見かけることができる。
== 成分 == 湯の花には[[硫黄]]、[[カルシウム]]、[[アルミニウム]]、[[鉄]]、[[珪素]]など様々な元素が含まれる。湯の花は源泉の泉質によって成分が異なり、主成分に応じて硫黄華、硫酸塩華、[[石灰岩#化学的沈殿|石灰華]]、珪華などに分類される{{Sfn|上野ら(2008)}}。例えば高温強酸性の[[草津温泉]]では硫黄華、様々な泉質の[[別府温泉]]では硫黄華、硫酸塩華、珪華の3種類が得られる。
== 採取方法 == 天然の湯の花の採取にはいくつかの方法がある。
=== 別府明礬温泉の湯の花 === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|明礬温泉の湯の花小屋]] [[大分県]][[別府市]]の[[別府温泉#明礬温泉|明礬温泉]]では、[[地熱]]地帯に「湯の花小屋」と呼ばれるわらぶき小屋を建て、小屋の中に青[[粘土]]を敷き詰め、粘土から析出し[[結晶]]化した湯の花や[[ミョウバン]]（明礬）を収穫する方法が採られている<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>。この方法により製造される薬用湯の花は、全国で唯一[[医薬部外品]]指定であり生産量も多く全国に広く流通している。
==== 湯の花の生成と採取 ==== 地熱地帯より噴出した硫気ガスは、[[硫化水素]]や[[二酸化硫黄]]を含んでいる。これらが酸素に触れると過酸化硫黄となり、さらに[[水蒸気]]と反応して[[硫酸]]となる。敷き詰められた青粘土の表面付近では硫酸の濃度が上昇し、それに伴って粘土中の[[鉄]]や[[アルミニウム]]が溶出されてくる。これがさらに乾燥すると、アルミニウム硫酸塩（アルノーゲン）や鉄・アルミニウム硫酸塩（ハロトリカイト）を主成分とする湯の花が針状結晶として得られる{{Sfn|恒松(2005)}}{{Sfn|恒松(2007)}}。
青粘土を敷いてから5-6回程度は湯の花が採取できる。粘土中の鉄が減ってくるとアルノーゲンの割合が増し、さらにアルミニウムも不足すると[[二酸化ケイ素]]の析出が優占するようになる。こうして用済みとなった青粘土は廃棄され、新たな粘土と交換される{{Sfn|恒松(2005)}}{{Sfn|恒松(2007)}}。
==== 歴史 ==== この明礬の製造は、江戸時代の[[1664年]]（[[寛文]]4年）、渡辺五郎右衛門が[[森藩]]領の[[豊後国]][[速見郡]]鶴見村の照湯にて初めて成功した。[[1725年]]（[[享保]]10年）には脇儀助が同じ鶴見村の明礬温泉にて本格的な生産をおこない、さらに隣接する幕府領野田村でも生産が行われ、産出量はこれを合わせると全国の70%を占めた。火薬の原料にもなる明礬は、[[1730年]]（[[享保]]15年）からは幕府の専売品として明礬会所が設けられ、独占的な取引がおこなわれていた。[[明治時代]]以降、中国産の安価な明礬が流通するようになると、専ら湯の花が製造されるようになった<ref name="bunka00000814"/>。
別府で長年受け継がれてきたこの伝統的な湯の花製造技術は、[[1968年]]（昭和43年）に「'''別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術'''」として別府市指定の[[無形文化財]]に指定され、[[2006年]]（平成18年）には同名で国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]にも指定されている<ref>指定年月日：2006年3月15日、保護団体名：明礬温泉湯の花製造技術保存会</ref><ref name="bunka00000814"/>。
=== 草津温泉の湯の花 === もう一つは、[[草津温泉]]の[[湯畑]]のように木製の[[樋]]に源泉を通して行う方法である。およそ2ヶ月ほど源泉を樋に流し、湯の花を析出させる。その後源泉を樋ごとに堰き止め、湯の花を採取する。それを乾燥室で乾燥させ、乾燥後、容器に詰め商品とする。
草津温泉の人工湯の花は、元来、硫黄鉱山原石粉砕湯花（天然）と西の河原源泉むしろ沈殿乾燥計り売り湯花（天然）が由来である。[[水道]]水の普及により、購入者から「強すぎる」という声が多く出て、塩素を除去・弱化させる炭酸カルシウムで天然原料の時代から希釈して、皮膚弱者を守ってきた経緯がある。
「草津温泉誌」では草津温泉湯畑の湯の花が「湯花稼」として紹介され、近隣硫黄鉱山（白根・万座・石津・[[小串鉱山|小串]]・吾妻など）で採集された硫黄が「硫黄稼」として紹介されている。硫黄鉱山は昭和40年代後半には近隣5鉱山とも姿を消して、脱硫精製化学硫黄に成分が変更された。業社は[[群馬県庁]]に人工化された新製品のサンプルを持参し、相談をしたが「法的に販売を禁止するべきものではない」という県側の見解を当時得た。しかし許認可権に関わる見解ではなかったので、この記録は群馬県庁には残っていない。
2006年末に、草津温泉地区で天然の湯の花（湯畑で採集されたもの）として販売されていた湯の花が、実際には[[原油]]から製造された硫黄に炭酸カルシウムを混ぜたものであったことが判明し、公正取引委員会から排除命令を受けた<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jftc.go.jp/pressrelease/06.december/061214.html |title=草津温泉地区における入浴剤販売業者4社に対する排除命令について |access-date=2023-05-25 |publisher=公正取引委員会 |date=2006-12-14 |archive-url=https://warp.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/286894/www.jftc.go.jp/pressrelease/06.december/061214.html |archive-date=2009-07-16 |url-status=dead|url-status-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>。しかしその後も、人工の入浴剤がパッケージを変更して販売されているのではないかという指摘がある<ref name="kusatsu1">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.town.kusatsu.gunma.jp/www/contents/1226575014391/index.html |title=草津温泉「湯の花」御愛顧の皆々様へ |access-date=2023-05-25 |publisher=草津町 |archive-url=https://warp.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1079437/www.town.kusatsu.gunma.jp/www/contents/1226575014391/index.html |archive-date=2010-07-03 |url-status=dead|url-status-date=2010-11-18 |date=2007-07-24}}</ref>。なお、2017年3月現在、草津町が販売している湯の花は、上面に赤字で「草津温泉 湯の花」と印字され、発売元が「群馬県草津町」と表示されている円柱状のプラスチック容器に入ったもの（内容量：95g・小売価格：1,400円）である<ref name="kusatsu2">{{Cite web|和書|title=湯の花の容器の変更について |url=https://www.town.kusatsu.gunma.jp/www/contents/1486521380919/index.html |access-date=2023-05-25 |publisher=草津町 |date=2017-03-21}}</ref>。これは年間約5,000個ほどしか販売されていない<ref name="kusatsu1"/>。 == 利用 == 湯の花はその硫黄分を生かし、古くは[[ハチ]]の巣の下で燃やして蜂の巣・蜂の子採りに使われたり、[[モグラ]]の穴にかけてモグラよけ、林業の毒虫よけなど忌避物質として利用された。他にも[[火薬]]や[[電柱]]の[[碍子]]の中身、[[ガラス]]研磨といった無機的な利用や、[[かんぴょう]]の漂白、[[漬物]]の味調整、治療薬などとしての利用法があった。
[[入浴剤]]として用いる場合、単体の[[硫黄]]や金属の[[硫化物]]を含む湯の花は風呂釜を傷める。対して、[[炭酸カルシウム]]や[[硫酸ナトリウム]]、[[硫酸カルシウム]]などを主成分とする湯の花は腐食性が低い。そのため、[[ボイラー]]などによる追い焚き機能を有した浴槽で湯の花を使いたい場合は、その成分を事前に確認する必要がある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？ |url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1 |access-date=2023-05-25 |publisher=ヤングビーナス薬品工業株式会社}}</ref>。
== 取り扱い上の注意 == {{出典の明記|section=1|date=2010年8月}} {{law|section=1}} 硫黄の含量が高く発火性が強い湯花は、20kg以上の保管・運搬において、都道府県火災予防条例で保管の方法に規制が設けられている。発火・炎上を避けるために、硫黄系天然湯の花を半乾燥の団子状態で販売する例もある（栃木県[[那須温泉郷#那須湯本温泉|那須湯元温泉]]など）。また発火性・[[引火点|引火性]]が強い湯花は、[[消防法]]の規定により、100kg以上の保管・運搬において、政府の法令の規定を受ける。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{cite journal |和書 | author = 恒松栖 | title = 別府における伝統産業湯の花 | journal = 別府大学短期大学部紀要 | year = 2005 | volume = 24 | pages = 1-11 | naid = 110006141439 | url = http://repo.beppu-u.ac.jp/modules/xoonips/detail.php?id=tk02404 |ref={{harvid|恒松(2005)}} }} * {{cite journal |和書 | author = 一國雅巳、加藤暢浩、大谷大二郎 | title = 別府温泉明礬地区における湯の花の生成 : 化学的考察 | journal = [http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number.html 温泉科学] | year = 2009 | volume = 59 | issue = 2 | pages = 88-96 | naid = 10025546106 | id={{NDLJP|8747631}} | url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol59_pdf/vol59no2_088_096.pdf | format=PDF |ref=harv }} * {{cite journal |和書 | author = 恒松栖 | title = 湯の花小屋のひみつ | journal = 別府史談 | year = 2007 | volume = 20 | pages = 53-65 | naid = 120001798443 | url = http://bud.beppu-u.ac.jp/modules/xoonips/detail.php?id=bs02008 |ref={{harvid|恒松(2007)}} }} * {{cite journal |和書 | author = 上野禎一、寺山亜沙実、向野美由紀、秦照美、岸本典子 | title = 湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1 | journal = 福岡教育大学紀要 | url = https://hdl.handle.net/10780/257 | year = 2008 | volume = 57 | issue = 3 | pages = 25-40 | naid = 120006890959 |ref={{harvid|上野ら(2008)}} }}
== 関連項目 == * [[トラバーチン]] * [[水の華]] * [[波の花]]

{{DEFAULTSORT:ゆのはな}} [[Category:温泉]] [[Category:硫黄]] [[Category:入浴]] [[Category:医薬部外品]] [[Category:別府市]] [[Category:草津町]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[湯の花]] to [[Yunohana]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|[[那須温泉郷]][[殺生石]]近郊産の湯の花（源泉付近の含硫黄堆積物）。黄色い部分は硫黄の斑晶。]] '''湯の花'''（ゆのはな）とは、[[温泉]]の不溶性成分が[[析出]]・[[沈殿]]したものを指す{{Sfn|上野ら(2008)}}。「湯の花」以外にも、湯花、湯の華、湯華など、複数の表記がある。一般に[[入浴剤]]などの用途で採取・販売されている。
== 概要 == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|湯の花採取光景（湯畑）（[[玉川温泉 (秋田県)|玉川温泉]]）]] 高温で湧出した[[源泉]]が大気に接触すると、温度差による冷却（[[溶解度]]の減少）、[[溶媒]]成分の蒸発、[[酸素]]との反応などにより源泉中の温泉成分や混在していた物質の沈殿が発生する。この不溶性沈殿が湯の花である{{Sfn|上野ら(2008)}}。析出物沈殿物は、粒子状になって浴槽の底に沈んだり、糸状になって浴槽内を漂ったり、浴槽の壁面や湯口に付着する。浴槽内を漂う湯の花は汚れと誤解されることもあるため、注意書きを掲示している温泉施設もある。<!--[[File:Owani Onsen Aomori 02.jpg|thumb|湯の花の注意書き（[[大鰐温泉]]）]]←この写真は言語の著作物に対する権利侵害にあたるのではないか。-->
また、湯の花は、温泉地においては温泉成分の沈殿物のみならず、硫黄鉱山原石粉砕品、源泉湧出地の石粉砕品、噴煙等粘土かぶせ湯花（別府温泉・奥塩原温泉が有名）、[[蒸気]]が付着したものなど多数の可能性がある。
[[北海道]]にある[[オンネトー]]のマンガンや、[[富山県]]にある[[新湯]]の玉滴石（[[オパール]]）など、湯の花が「生きている鉱床」となることがある。
湯の花を集めて包装したものは、温泉地における[[土産]]の定番として広く流通しており、多くの[[温泉街]]で見かけることができる。
== 成分 == 湯の花には[[硫黄]]、[[カルシウム]]、[[アルミニウム]]、[[鉄]]、[[珪素]]など様々な元素が含まれる。湯の花は源泉の泉質によって成分が異なり、主成分に応じて硫黄華、硫酸塩華、[[石灰岩#化学的沈殿|石灰華]]、珪華などに分類される{{Sfn|上野ら(2008)}}。例えば高温強酸性の[[草津温泉]]では硫黄華、様々な泉質の[[別府温泉]]では硫黄華、硫酸塩華、珪華の3種類が得られる。
== 採取方法 == 天然の湯の花の採取にはいくつかの方法がある。
=== 別府明礬温泉の湯の花 === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|明礬温泉の湯の花小屋]] [[大分県]][[別府市]]の[[別府温泉#明礬温泉|明礬温泉]]では、[[地熱]]地帯に「湯の花小屋」と呼ばれるわらぶき小屋を建て、小屋の中に青[[粘土]]を敷き詰め、粘土から析出し[[結晶]]化した湯の花や[[ミョウバン]]（明礬）を収穫する方法が採られている<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>。この方法により製造される薬用湯の花は、全国で唯一[[医薬部外品]]指定であり生産量も多く全国に広く流通している。
==== 湯の花の生成と採取 ==== 地熱地帯より噴出した硫気ガスは、[[硫化水素]]や[[二酸化硫黄]]を含んでいる。これらが酸素に触れると過酸化硫黄となり、さらに[[水蒸気]]と反応して[[硫酸]]となる。敷き詰められた青粘土の表面付近では硫酸の濃度が上昇し、それに伴って粘土中の[[鉄]]や[[アルミニウム]]が溶出されてくる。これがさらに乾燥すると、アルミニウム硫酸塩（アルノーゲン）や鉄・アルミニウム硫酸塩（ハロトリカイト）を主成分とする湯の花が針状結晶として得られる{{Sfn|恒松(2005)}}{{Sfn|恒松(2007)}}。
青粘土を敷いてから5-6回程度は湯の花が採取できる。粘土中の鉄が減ってくるとアルノーゲンの割合が増し、さらにアルミニウムも不足すると[[二酸化ケイ素]]の析出が優占するようになる。こうして用済みとなった青粘土は廃棄され、新たな粘土と交換される{{Sfn|恒松(2005)}}{{Sfn|恒松(2007)}}。
==== 歴史 ==== この明礬の製造は、江戸時代の[[1664年]]（[[寛文]]4年）、渡辺五郎右衛門が[[森藩]]領の[[豊後国]][[速見郡]]鶴見村の照湯にて初めて成功した。[[1725年]]（[[享保]]10年）には脇儀助が同じ鶴見村の明礬温泉にて本格的な生産をおこない、さらに隣接する幕府領野田村でも生産が行われ、産出量はこれを合わせると全国の70%を占めた。火薬の原料にもなる明礬は、[[1730年]]（[[享保]]15年）からは幕府の専売品として明礬会所が設けられ、独占的な取引がおこなわれていた。[[明治時代]]以降、中国産の安価な明礬が流通するようになると、専ら湯の花が製造されるようになった<ref name="bunka00000814"/>。
別府で長年受け継がれてきたこの伝統的な湯の花製造技術は、[[1968年]]（昭和43年）に「'''別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術'''」として別府市指定の[[無形文化財]]に指定され、[[2006年]]（平成18年）には同名で国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]にも指定されている<ref>指定年月日：2006年3月15日、保護団体名：明礬温泉湯の花製造技術保存会</ref><ref name="bunka00000814"/>。
=== 草津温泉の湯の花 === もう一つは、[[草津温泉]]の[[湯畑]]のように木製の[[樋]]に源泉を通して行う方法である。およそ2ヶ月ほど源泉を樋に流し、湯の花を析出させる。その後源泉を樋ごとに堰き止め、湯の花を採取する。それを乾燥室で乾燥させ、乾燥後、容器に詰め商品とする。
草津温泉の人工湯の花は、元来、硫黄鉱山原石粉砕湯花（天然）と西の河原源泉むしろ沈殿乾燥計り売り湯花（天然）が由来である。[[水道]]水の普及により、購入者から「強すぎる」という声が多く出て、塩素を除去・弱化させる炭酸カルシウムで天然原料の時代から希釈して、皮膚弱者を守ってきた経緯がある。
「草津温泉誌」では草津温泉湯畑の湯の花が「湯花稼」として紹介され、近隣硫黄鉱山（白根・万座・石津・[[小串鉱山|小串]]・吾妻など）で採集された硫黄が「硫黄稼」として紹介されている。硫黄鉱山は昭和40年代後半には近隣5鉱山とも姿を消して、脱硫精製化学硫黄に成分が変更された。業社は[[群馬県庁]]に人工化された新製品のサンプルを持参し、相談をしたが「法的に販売を禁止するべきものではない」という県側の見解を当時得た。しかし許認可権に関わる見解ではなかったので、この記録は群馬県庁には残っていない。
2006年末に、草津温泉地区で天然の湯の花（湯畑で採集されたもの）として販売されていた湯の花が、実際には[[原油]]から製造された硫黄に炭酸カルシウムを混ぜたものであったことが判明し、公正取引委員会から排除命令を受けた<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jftc.go.jp/pressrelease/06.december/061214.html |title=草津温泉地区における入浴剤販売業者4社に対する排除命令について |access-date=2023-05-25 |publisher=公正取引委員会 |date=2006-12-14 |archive-url=https://warp.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/286894/www.jftc.go.jp/pressrelease/06.december/061214.html |archive-date=2009-07-16 |url-status=dead|url-status-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>。しかしその後も、人工の入浴剤がパッケージを変更して販売されているのではないかという指摘がある<ref name="kusatsu1">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.town.kusatsu.gunma.jp/www/contents/1226575014391/index.html |title=草津温泉「湯の花」御愛顧の皆々様へ |access-date=2023-05-25 |publisher=草津町 |archive-url=https://warp.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1079437/www.town.kusatsu.gunma.jp/www/contents/1226575014391/index.html |archive-date=2010-07-03 |url-status=dead|url-status-date=2010-11-18 |date=2007-07-24}}</ref>。なお、2017年3月現在、草津町が販売している湯の花は、上面に赤字で「草津温泉 湯の花」と印字され、発売元が「群馬県草津町」と表示されている円柱状のプラスチック容器に入ったもの（内容量：95g・小売価格：1,400円）である<ref name="kusatsu2">{{Cite web|和書|title=湯の花の容器の変更について |url=https://www.town.kusatsu.gunma.jp/www/contents/1486521380919/index.html |access-date=2023-05-25 |publisher=草津町 |date=2017-03-21}}</ref>。これは年間約5,000個ほどしか販売されていない<ref name="kusatsu1"/>。 == 利用 == 湯の花はその硫黄分を生かし、古くは[[ハチ]]の巣の下で燃やして蜂の巣・蜂の子採りに使われたり、[[モグラ]]の穴にかけてモグラよけ、林業の毒虫よけなど忌避物質として利用された。他にも[[火薬]]や[[電柱]]の[[碍子]]の中身、[[ガラス]]研磨といった無機的な利用や、[[かんぴょう]]の漂白、[[漬物]]の味調整、治療薬などとしての利用法があった。
[[入浴剤]]として用いる場合、単体の[[硫黄]]や金属の[[硫化物]]を含む湯の花は風呂釜を傷める。対して、[[炭酸カルシウム]]や[[硫酸ナトリウム]]、[[硫酸カルシウム]]などを主成分とする湯の花は腐食性が低い。そのため、[[ボイラー]]などによる追い焚き機能を有した浴槽で湯の花を使いたい場合は、その成分を事前に確認する必要がある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？ |url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1 |access-date=2023-05-25 |publisher=ヤングビーナス薬品工業株式会社}}</ref>。
== 取り扱い上の注意 == {{出典の明記|section=1|date=2010年8月}} {{law|section=1}} 硫黄の含量が高く発火性が強い湯花は、20kg以上の保管・運搬において、都道府県火災予防条例で保管の方法に規制が設けられている。発火・炎上を避けるために、硫黄系天然湯の花を半乾燥の団子状態で販売する例もある（栃木県[[那須温泉郷#那須湯本温泉|那須湯元温泉]]など）。また発火性・[[引火点|引火性]]が強い湯花は、[[消防法]]の規定により、100kg以上の保管・運搬において、政府の法令の規定を受ける。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{cite journal |和書 | author = 恒松栖 | title = 別府における伝統産業湯の花 | journal = 別府大学短期大学部紀要 | year = 2005 | volume = 24 | pages = 1-11 | naid = 110006141439 | url = http://repo.beppu-u.ac.jp/modules/xoonips/detail.php?id=tk02404 |ref={{harvid|恒松(2005)}} }} * {{cite journal |和書 | author = 一國雅巳、加藤暢浩、大谷大二郎 | title = 別府温泉明礬地区における湯の花の生成 : 化学的考察 | journal = [http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number.html 温泉科学] | year = 2009 | volume = 59 | issue = 2 | pages = 88-96 | naid = 10025546106 | id={{NDLJP|8747631}} | url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol59_pdf/vol59no2_088_096.pdf | format=PDF |ref=harv }} * {{cite journal |和書 | author = 恒松栖 | title = 湯の花小屋のひみつ | journal = 別府史談 | year = 2007 | volume = 20 | pages = 53-65 | naid = 120001798443 | url = http://bud.beppu-u.ac.jp/modules/xoonips/detail.php?id=bs02008 |ref={{harvid|恒松(2007)}} }} * {{cite journal |和書 | author = 上野禎一、寺山亜沙実、向野美由紀、秦照美、岸本典子 | title = 湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1 | journal = 福岡教育大学紀要 | url = https://hdl.handle.net/10780/257 | year = 2008 | volume = 57 | issue = 3 | pages = 25-40 | naid = 120006890959 |ref={{harvid|上野ら(2008)}} }}
== 関連項目 == * [[トラバーチン]] * [[水の華]] * [[波の花]]

revision.text:
[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|qq=draft|qq=draft}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|1=Onsen|1=Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|qq=draft|qq=draft}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|2=ja|3=温泉街|4=fr|5=Station thermale|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|1=spa town|lt=Onsen town|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}} == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>

== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}} == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|qq=draft|qq=draft}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|1=Onsen|1=Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|2=ja|3=温泉街|4=fr|5=Station thermale|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|1=spa town|lt=Onsen town|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}

The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|1=Onsen|1=Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>

These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|2=ja|3=温泉街|4=fr|5=Station thermale|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|1=spa town|lt=Onsen town|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.

== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}} == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

[[en:Yunohana]] [[ja:湯の花]]
<!--Yunohana--><!--Yunohana-->

[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|qq=draft|qq=draft}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|1=Onsen|1=Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|qq=draft|qq=draft}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|2=ja|3=温泉街|4=fr|5=Station thermale|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|1=spa town|lt=Onsen town|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}} == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Hot Spring|qq=}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/source water|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Mouth|qq=}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|qq=}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|qq=}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lake Onnetō|qq=}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|qq=}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|2=ja|3=温泉街|4=fr|5=Station thermale|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|1=spa town|lt=Onsen town|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/spa town|13=User:Immanuelle/Spa town|qq=}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusatsu Onsen|qq=}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|qq=}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|qq=}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Prefecture|qq=}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusatsu Onsen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusatsu Onsen|qq=}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}} == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Travertine|qq=}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Algal bloom|qq=}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sea foam|qq=}}

[[en:Yunohana]] [[ja:湯の花]]
<!--Yunohana--><!--Yunohana-->

[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu Onsen|qq=}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sessho-seki|qq=}}近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|1=Onsen|1=Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen|qq=}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Hot Spring|qq=}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/source water|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Mouth|qq=}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|qq=}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|qq=}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|ja|オンネトー|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lake Onnetō|qq=}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|qq=}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|spa town|lt=spa town|lt=onsen towns|ja|温泉街|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|2=ja|3=温泉街|4=fr|5=Station thermale|1=Spa town|lt=Onsen town|1=spa town|lt=Onsen town|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/spa town|13=User:Immanuelle/Spa town|qq=}}.
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusatsu Onsen|qq=}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|qq=}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|qq=}} in {{ill|Beppu|ja|別府市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Prefecture|qq=}}, "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusatsu Onsen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusatsu Onsen|qq=}}, yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}} == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|ja|トラバーチン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Travertine|qq=}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|ja|水の華|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Algal bloom|qq=}} * {{ill|Sea foam|ja|波の花|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sea foam|qq=}}
{{Onsen-stub}}
{{Onsen}}
{{translated page|ja|湯の花|version=101825924|comment=Imported full ja history}}

In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|qq=}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at [[:en:Lake Onnetō|Lake Onnetō]] in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|qq=}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many [[:en:Spa town|Onsen town]].
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] yield sulfur deposits, while [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|qq=}} in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]], yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.

The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Hot Spring|qq=}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/source water|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Mouth|qq=}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|qq=}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at [[:en:Lake Onnetō|Lake Onnetō]] in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|qq=}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many [[:en:Spa town|Onsen town]].
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] yield sulfur deposits, while [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==

Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]], yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>

<!--Yunohana--><!--Yunohana--> [[Category:Beppu, Ōita]] [[Category:医薬部外品]] [[Category:Hot springs]] [[Category:Kusatsu, Gunma]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh_yue:湯花]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove invalid enwiki interwiki links
revision.text:
[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu Onsen|qq=}} [[:en:Sessho-seki|Sessho-seki]]近郊産の yunohana (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Yunohana''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Yunohana is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Yunohana being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Hot Spring|qq=}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/source water|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Mouth|qq=}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are yunohana.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Yunohana can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating yunohana is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|qq=}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at [[:en:Lake Onnetō|Lake Onnetō]] in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|qq=}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many [[:en:Spa town|Onsen town]].
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] yield sulfur deposits, while [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|qq=}} in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]], yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}} == See Also == * [[:en:Travertine|Travertine]] * [[:en:Algal bloom|Algal bloom]] * [[:en:Sea foam|Sea foam]]

In addition to being deposits of onsen components, yunohana can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at [[:en:Lake Onnetō|Lake Onnetō]] in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|qq=}} in Toyama, yunohana becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged yunohana is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many [[:en:Spa town|Onsen town]].
== Composition == Yunohana contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of yunohana varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] yield sulfur deposits, while [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Yunohana at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The yunohana huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉}} in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], "yunohana huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where yunohana and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped yunohana deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield yunohana about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]], yunohana is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Yunohana has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich yunohana with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}} == See Also == * [[:en:Travertine|Travertine]] * [[:en:Algal bloom|Algal bloom]] * [[:en:Sea foam|Sea foam]]
