Packaged Sinter is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many [[:en:Spa town|Onsen town]].
== Composition == Sinter contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of Sinter varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] yield sulfur deposits, while [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Sinter at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The Sinter huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}} in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], "Sinter huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where Sinter and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped Sinter deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield Sinter about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]], Sinter is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Sinter has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich Sinter with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws. == See Also == * [[:en:Travertine|Travertine]] * [[:en:Algal bloom|Algal bloom]] * [[:en:Sea foam|Sea foam]]

{{Onsen}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11563426]] Wikidata name: Sinter [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu Onsen|1=Nasu Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642937}} [[:en:Sessho-seki|Sessho-seki]]近郊産の Sinter (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] '''Sinter''' (湯の花) refers to the insoluble components of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Sinter is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Sinter being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Hot Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572582}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/source water|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Mouth|qq=}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are Sinter.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Sinter can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating Sinter is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, Sinter can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at [[:en:Lake Onnetō|Lake Onnetō]] in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|1=Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85880600}} in Toyama, Sinter becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged Sinter is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many [[:en:Spa town|Onsen town]].
== Composition == Sinter contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of Sinter varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] yield sulfur deposits, while [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />

For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Sinter at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The Sinter huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}} in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture}}, "Sinter huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where Sinter and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped Sinter deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield Sinter about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}}, Sinter is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Sinter has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich Sinter with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws. == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|en|Travertine|WD=Q187156|lt=Travertine}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|en|Algal bloom|WD=Q326139|lt=Algal bloom}} * {{ill|Sea foam|en|Sea foam|WD=Q249020|lt=Sea foam}}
{{Onsen-stub}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}
{{Onsen}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11563426]] Wikidata name: Sinter [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu Onsen|1=Nasu Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642937}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki}}近郊産の Sinter (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] {{nihongo|'''Sinter'''|湯の花|Yunohana}} refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Sinter is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Sinter being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Hot Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572582}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/source water|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Mouth|qq=}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are Sinter.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Sinter can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating Sinter is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, Sinter can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|en|Lake Onnetō|WD=Q3215112|lt=Lake Onnetō}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|1=Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85880600}} in Toyama, Sinter becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged Sinter is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|Spa town|en|Spa town|WD=Q4946461|lt=Onsen town}}.
== Composition == Sinter contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of Sinter varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Sinter at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The Sinter huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}} in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture}}, "Sinter huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where Sinter and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped Sinter deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield Sinter about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}}, Sinter is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Sinter has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich Sinter with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws. == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|en|Travertine|WD=Q187156|lt=Travertine}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|en|Algal bloom|WD=Q326139|lt=Algal bloom}} * {{ill|Sea foam|en|Sea foam|WD=Q249020|lt=Sea foam}}
{{Onsen-stub}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}

Packaged Sinter is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|Spa town|en|Spa town|WD=Q4946461|lt=Onsen town}}.
== Composition == Sinter contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of Sinter varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Sinter at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The Sinter huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}} in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture}}, "Sinter huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where Sinter and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped Sinter deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield Sinter about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}}, Sinter is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Sinter has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich Sinter with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws. == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|en|Travertine|WD=Q187156|lt=Travertine}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|en|Algal bloom|WD=Q326139|lt=Algal bloom}} * {{ill|Sea foam|en|Sea foam|WD=Q249020|lt=Sea foam}}
{{Onsen-stub}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}
{{Onsen}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]]
[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu Onsen|1=Nasu Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642937}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki}}近郊産の Sinter (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] {{nihongo|'''Sinter'''|湯の花|Yunohana}} refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Sinter is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Sinter being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Hot Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572582}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/source water|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Mouth|qq=}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are Sinter.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Sinter can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating Sinter is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, Sinter can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|en|Lake Onnetō|WD=Q3215112|lt=Lake Onnetō}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|1=Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85880600}} in Toyama, Sinter becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged Sinter is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|Spa town|en|Spa town|WD=Q4946461|lt=Onsen town}}.
== Composition == Sinter contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of Sinter varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Sinter at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The Sinter huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}} in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture}}, "Sinter huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where Sinter and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped Sinter deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield Sinter about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}}, Sinter is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Sinter has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>

Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws. == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|en|Travertine|WD=Q187156|lt=Travertine}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|en|Algal bloom|WD=Q326139|lt=Algal bloom}} * {{ill|Sea foam|en|Sea foam|WD=Q249020|lt=Sea foam}}
{{Onsen-stub}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=恒松栖|title=湯の花小屋のひみつ|journal=別府史談|year=2007|volume=20|pages=53-65}} * {{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}

Floating Sinter is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, Sinter can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|en|Lake Onnetō|WD=Q3215112|lt=Lake Onnetō|1=Lake Onnetō}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|1=Q85880600|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85880600}} in Toyama, Sinter becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged Sinter is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|Spa town|en|Spa town|WD=Q4946461|lt=Onsen town|1=Spa town}}.
== Composition == Sinter contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of Sinter varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen|1=Kusatsu Onsen}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
== Harvesting Methods ==
=== Sinter at Myoban Onsen === [[File:Myoban Onsen 02.jpg|thumb|The Sinter huts of {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}]] At the {{ill|Myoban Onsen|ja|明礬温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoban Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}} in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu|1=Beppu}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, "Sinter huts" made of straw are constructed in geothermal areas. Blue clay is spread inside the huts, where Sinter and alum (明礬) crystallize and can be harvested.<ref name="bunka00000814">[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/00000814 別府明礬温泉の湯の花製造技術] - 国指定文化財等データベース</ref>
=== Generation and Harvesting Process === Sulfurous gases emitted from geothermal areas contain hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide. These gases react with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, which further reacts with water vapor to create sulfuric acid. As the sulfuric acid concentration increases on the surface of the blue clay, aluminum and iron dissolve, eventually crystallizing as needle-shaped Sinter deposits.<ref name="恒松(2005)">恒松栖, 湯の花小屋のひみつ, 別府史談, 2005, 24, pp. 1-11</ref>
The blue clay can yield Sinter about five to six times before it is replaced due to depletion of aluminum and iron content.
=== Historical Context === The manufacturing of alum at Myoban Onsen began in 1664 (Kanbun 4) under the efforts of Watanabe Gorōemon. By the 18th century, it became a major industry, and in 2006, the traditional manufacturing technique was designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.<ref name="bunka00000814"/>
=== Kusatsu Onsen === At {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen|1=Kusatsu Onsen}}, Sinter is harvested by channeling the source water through wooden conduits for approximately two months. The deposits are then collected, dried, and packaged for sale.
== Uses == Sinter has historically been used for various purposes, such as deterring pests and as a component in explosives, glass polishing, and traditional medicines. As a bath additive, its composition (e.g., sulfur vs. calcium carbonate) determines whether it is suitable for modern heating systems.<ref>{{Cite web|title=湯の花は風呂釜を傷めるイオウじゃないのですか？|url=https://www.youngvenus.co.jp/faqs.html#flush-headingE-1|access-date=2023-05-25}}</ref>
== Handling Precautions == Sulfur-rich Sinter with high flammability is regulated under fire prevention ordinances. Storage and transport of more than 20 kg require compliance with specific regulations, while over 100 kg may be subject to national fire laws. == See Also == * {{ill|Travertine|en|Travertine|WD=Q187156|lt=Travertine|1=Travertine}} * {{ill|Algal bloom|en|Algal bloom|WD=Q326139|lt=Algal bloom|1=Algal bloom}} * {{ill|Sea foam|en|Sea foam|WD=Q249020|lt=Sea foam|1=Sea foam}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湯の花]][[zh-yue:湯花]]
[[File:Yunohana1.jpg|thumb|300px|{{ill|Nasu Onsen|ja|那須温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu Onsen|1=Nasu Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642937}} {{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki|1=Sessho-seki}}近郊産の Sinter (sulfurous deposits near the source). The yellow areas are sulfur phenocrysts.]] {{nihongo|'''Sinter'''|湯の花|Yunohana}} refers to the insoluble components of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}} that precipitate and settle out of the water. There are multiple written forms of the name, including 湯花, 湯の華, and 湯華. Sinter is often harvested and sold for use in bath additives.<ref name="上野ら(2008)">{{Cite journal|author=上野禎一|title=湯の花の鉱物学的研究、その1|journal=福岡教育大学紀要|year=2008|volume=57|issue=3|pages=25-40}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Tamagawa Onsen Akita 03.jpg|thumb|Sinter being harvested at the {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|ja|玉川温泉|1=Tamagawa Hot Spring|lt=Tamagawa Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Onsen (Akita Prefecture)|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagawa Hot Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572582}}.]] When high-temperature {{ill|source water|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/source water|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Mouth|qq=}} emerges and comes into contact with the air, cooling (reducing solubility), evaporation of solvent, or reactions with oxygen may cause precipitation of the dissolved components in the source water. These insoluble precipitates are Sinter.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" /> Sinter can settle at the bottom of a bathtub as particles, float as thread-like forms in the water, or attach to the walls and spouts of the bath. Floating Sinter is sometimes mistaken for dirt, prompting some onsen facilities to post notices to clarify its nature.
In addition to being deposits of onsen components, Sinter can also form from sulfur mine pulverized raw materials, powdered stone from spring outlets, or fumaroles mixed with clay (notable examples include {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen}} and {{ill|Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja|奥塩原温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Shiobara Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
At certain locations, such as the manganese deposits at {{ill|Lake Onnetō|en|Lake Onnetō|WD=Q3215112|lt=Lake Onnetō|1=Lake Onnetō}} in Hokkaido or the opal deposits at {{ill|Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|ja|新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Toyama Prefecture)|1=Q85880600|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85880600}} in Toyama, Sinter becomes a "living ore deposit."
Packaged Sinter is a common souvenir at onsen resorts and is widely available in many {{ill|Spa town|en|Spa town|WD=Q4946461|lt=Onsen town|1=Spa town}}.
== Composition == Sinter contains various elements, such as sulfur, calcium, aluminum, iron, and silicon. The composition of Sinter varies depending on the source's {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|Hot Spring Types=Hot Spring Types|lt=onsen composition|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}, and it is classified based on its primary components, such as sulfur deposits (硫黄華), sulfate deposits (硫酸塩華), travertine (石灰華), or siliceous sinter (珪華). For instance, the high-temperature, highly acidic waters at {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen|1=Kusatsu Onsen}} yield sulfur deposits, while {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen}} produces a mix of sulfur, sulfate, and siliceous sinter deposits.<ref name="上野ら(2008)" />
